cards.text: About 118 elements are known today. There are more than 90 metals,22 non metals and a few metalloids.
Oxygen(O), hydrogen(H), nitrogen(N), sulphur(S), phosphorus(P), fluorine(F), chlorine(Cl), bromine(Br), iodine(l) are some non-metals.

Physical Properties of Non-Metals:

  • Non-metals are either in the solid or gaseous state.
    Exception: Bromine is an exception which exists in the liquid state.
  • Non-metals do not have lustre.
    Exception: Iodine crystals are lustrous.
  • They do not possess the property of hardness.
    Exception: Carbon in the form of diamond is the hardest substance, which has a high melting and boiling point.
  • Non-metals are poor conductors of heat and electricity.
    Exception: Graphite, an allotrope of carbon which conducts electricity.
Metalloids are elements which behave like both metals and non -metals. Eg. Boron, Silicon, Arsenic
Uses of non-metal
Uses of metal

Metals are the elements which form cations (positive ions) by losing electrons, so they are electropositive in nature. Metals generally have one to three electrons in their valence shells. Eg. Gold, Silver, Iron.
Some elements show the properties of both metals and non-metals and are called metalloids.
Metals form basic oxides e.g., Magnesium oxide (MgO), while non-metals form acidic oxides e.g., SO2,CO2.
Sodium (Na), potassium (K), magnesium(Mg), aluminium(Al),calcium(Ca), Iron(Fe), Barium(Ba) are some metals.

Non-Metals are the elements which form anions (negative ions) by gaining electrons, so they are electronegative in nature. Non-Metals usually have four to eight electrons in their valence shells. Eg. Carbon, Bromine, Oxygen

Physical Properties of Metals:

  • Physical State: All metals are solids at room temperature.
    Exceptions: Mercury and gallium are liquids at room temperature.
  • Luster: All metals in their pure state have a shine and can be polished to give a highly reflective surface.
  • Malleability: Metals have the ability to withstand high tensile strength and can be made into thin sheets. This property of metals is called malleability.
  • Ductility: Metals can also be drawn into thin wires. The ability of metals to be drawn into wires is called ductility. Example: Gold and silver are the most ductile metals.
  • Conduction of Heat: Metals are good conductors of heat and have high melting points. Example: Silver and copper are very good conductors of electricity.
    Exceptions: Lead and mercury are poor conductors of heat.
  • Conduction of Electricity: Metals are good conductors of electricity.
    Example: All electric wires are made of copper.

Difference between metal and non metal

Metals Non-Metals 
Solid at room temperature except mercury Exist in all the three states, bromine is liquid
Ductile and malleable Non-ductile and non-malleable
Sonorous and lustrous  Non-sonorous and non-lustrous except Iodine and graphite
Generally good conductors of heat and electricity except lead and mercury  Poor conductors, except graphite
Have high density but sodium and potassium have low density Have low density
Metal oxides can be basic or amphoteric in nature Oxides of non-metals are acidic generally in nature
Many metals displace hydrogen from dilute acids and release hydrogen gas.  Non metal cannot displace hydrogen from dilute acids
Metal oxides are ionic in nature Non metal oxides are covalent in nature
The arrangement of metals in the order of decreasing reactivities is called the reactivity series of metals.

Reactions of Metals with Acids: Metals react with acids to form salt and hydrogen gas.
Metal + Dilute acid {tex}\to{/tex} Salt + Hydrogen
Mg(s) + 2HCl(aq) {tex}\to{/tex} MgCl2(aq) + H2(g)
Fe and Cu don’t burn in air but combine with oxygen to form oxide. Iron filings burn when sprinkled over flame.
Some metals like Mg, Al, Zn, Pb react slowly with air and form a protective layer.
Some metals like Na and K are kept immersed in kerosene oil as they react vigorously with air and catch fire.
Chemical Properties of Metals Reaction with air: Different metals show different reactivities towards oxygen present in air. Metal + oxygen {tex}\to{/tex} Metal Oxide.

Reaction of Metals with Oxygen: Almost all metals react with oxygen to form metal oxides.
Metal + Oxygen {tex}\to{/tex} Metal oxide
{tex}\underset{(Metal)}{\underset{Sodium}{4\mathrm{Na}(\mathrm s)}}+\underset{(Fromair)}{\underset{Oxygen}{{\mathrm O}_2(\;\mathrm g)}}\rightarrow\underset{(Basicoxide)}{\underset{Sodium\;oxide}{2{\mathrm{Na}}_2\mathrm O(\mathrm s)}}\\{/tex}

Reaction of Metals with Water: Metals react with water to produce metal oxides with the release of hydrogen gas. But all metals do not react with water.
Metal + Water {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Metal oxide + Hydrogen
Metal oxides that are soluble in water dissolve in it to further form metal hydroxide.
Metal oxide + Water {tex}\rightarrow{/tex} Metal hydroxide

Metals react with Solutions of other Metal Salts : A more reactive metal displaces a less reactive metal from its salt solution. For example: When an iron nail is placed in a copper sulphate solution, the blue colour of CuSO4 fades away slowly and a reddish brown copper metal is formed.
CuSO4(aq) + Fe(s) {tex}\to{/tex} FeSO4(aq) + Cu(s)

Reaction of Metals with Chlorine: Metals react with chlorine to form metal chlorides. For example: Sodium readily reacts with chlorine to form ionic chloride called sodium chloride.
2Na(s) + Cl2(g) {tex}\to{/tex} 2NaCl(s)

Uses of metals:

  1. Lead metal is used in making car batteries.
  2. Zinc is used for galvanizing iron to protect it from rusting.
  3. Iron, copper and aluminium are used to make utensils.
  4. Copper and aluminium metals are used to make wires.
  5. Aluminium is used to make aluminium foil for packaging materials.

Uses of non-metals:

  1. Hydrogen is used in the hydrogenation of vegetable oils.
  2. Carbon is used to make electrodes of electrolytic cells and dry cells.
  3. Nitrogen is used in the manufacture of ammonia, nitric acid and fertilizers.
  4. Sulphur is used in making sulphuric acid.

cards.text:
Metals Non-metals
Generally solid except Hg(present in liquid form).

It can be solid, liquid organs.

For examples: C is solid, Br (liquid), H(gas)
Ductile, Malleable (drawn into wires) (beaten into sheets) Non-ductile, non-Malleable
Sonorous (produces sound) Non-sonorous
Lustrous (have natural shine) Non-lustrous except for Iodine.

cards.text: Metals and Non-metals can be distinguished on the basis of their physical and chemical properties.

cards.text: Reactivity of elements is the tendency to attain a completely filled valence shell to achieve the nearest inert gas configuration.
cards.topic_name: How do Metals and Non-Metals react?

cards.text:
• Atoms of metals lose electrons from their valence shell to form cations. 
  • Atoms of non-metals gain electron in the valence shell to form anions.

cards.text:
Properties of Ionic Compounds:

  1. Ionic compounds are hard solids, due to the strong force of attraction between the positive and negative ions.
  2. They are generally brittle and break into pieces when pressure is applied.
  3. Ionic compounds have high melting and boiling points since a large amount of energy is required to break the strong intermolecular attractions.
  4. They are soluble in water but insoluble in solvents such as kerosene, petrol, etc.
  5. They do not conduct electricity in a solid state, because electrostatic forces of attraction between ions in the solid state are very strong but conduct electricity in the fused.
cards.topic_name: How do Metals and Non-Metals react?

cards.text: Minerals: Compounds present in earth's crust can be termed as minerals.
Ores: Mineral from which metal can be economically extract is called an ore. All minerals are not ores.
Metals at the bottom of reactivity series like gold, platinum, silver, copper occur in free state.
Gangue: Ores are found mixed with earthly impurities like soil, sand etc. known as gangue. The gangue is removed from the ore.

Metallurgy: It is the step-wise process of obtaining metal from its ore.
These steps are

  • Enrichment of ore.
  • Obtaining metal from enriched ore.
  • Refining of impure metal to obtain pure metal.

Extraction of Mercury Cinnabar, an ore of mercury is first heated in the air and is converted into mercuric oxide.
{tex}\underset{Mercury\;(II)\;Sulphide}{2\mathrm{HgS}(\mathrm s)}+\underset{Oxygen}{3{\mathrm O}_2}{/tex} {tex}\xrightarrow{\mathrm{Roasting}}\underset{Mercury\;(II)\;Sulphide}{2\mathrm{HgO}(\mathrm s)}+\underset{Sulphur\;dioxide}{2{\mathrm{SO}}_2(\mathrm g)}{/tex}
Mercuric oxide is then reduced to mercury on further heating.
{tex}\underset{Mercury\;(III)\;Oxide}{2\mathrm{HgO}(\mathrm s)}\xrightarrow[\mathrm{Reduction}]{\mathrm{Heat}}\underset{Mercury\;metal}{2\mathrm{Hg}(\mathrm l)}+\underset{Oxygen}{{\mathrm O}_2(\mathrm g)}{/tex}
Calcination is the process in which a carbonate ore is heated strongly in the absence of air to convert it into a metal oxide.
Metals high up in the reactivity series are very reactive. These metals have a strong affinity for oxygen. So, oxides of sodium, magnesium, calcium  and aluminium cannot be reduced by carbon.
Experimental set up for the electrolytic refining
When the surface of a metal is attacked by air, moisture or any other substance around it, the metal is said to corrode and the phenomenon is known as corrosion.

Conditions necessary for rusting of iron:

  • Presence of air (or oxygen)
  • Presence of water (or moisture)
Experimental set up for the electrolytic refining

Prevention of Corrosion:

  1. Galvanising: It is the process of giving coating a thin layer of zinc on iron or steel to protect them from corrosion. Example: shiny nails, pins. etc.
  2. Tinning: It is a process of coating tin over other metals.
  3. Electroplating: In this method, a metal is coated with another metal using electrolysis. Example: silver plated spoons, gold plated jewellery etc.
  4. Alloying: An alloy is a homogeneous mixture of two or more metals or a metal and a non-metal in a definite proportion. The resultant metals, called alloys do not corrode easily.
